methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $5M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 11 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
DIRAC, a seed-stage robotics and manufacturing company founded by Fil Aronshtein, has raised $5M+ to date. The broader sector comprises one public comparable and eight private peers including 1X Technologies, Divergent, Exotec, Locus Robotics, and Bedrock Robotics, though none provide sufficiently tight stage or valuation alignment for narrow peer-median analysis.

A single valuation method proved applicable: stage-progression analysis. Using the $5M total raised as a proxy for current value and applying a 2.5x step-up multiplier typical of seed-to-next-stage progression yields an implied valuation of $12M. The peer-median method was not executable; while 11 same-sector comparables exist, none met the threshold of three or more datapoints within same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-valuation-tier filters. A broad all-sector median would introduce excessive noise given the absence of meaningfully comparable seed-stage robotics companies with published valuations.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $11M (low) to $14M (high), with a median of $12M. This narrow band reflects reliance on a single methodology and the absence of direct peer comparables at this stage. Key uncertainties include the actual post-money valuation from the most recent financing round—which would refine the base value assumption—and the applicability of a 2.5x step-up multiplier to this specific company's profile and market conditions. Additional comparable transaction data from later-stage seed or Series A robotics companies would materially improve conviction in this range.
